title: Reinforcement Learning & RLHF
description: Reinforcement Learning fundamentals — MDPs, Q-learning, policy gradients, PPO, multi-armed bandits — and RLHF for LLM alignment, with FAANG interview depth.
details: RL covers the MDP formulation, Q-learning, policy gradients (PPO), and multi-armed bandits for exploration. RLHF extends this to align LLMs with human preferences — the technique behind ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. This guide covers the math, implementation, and FAANG interview angles.

sections.title: MDP & Bellman Equations
sections.description:
📋 The MDP Framework

MDP = ⟨S, A, P, R, γ⟩. State s ∈ S, action a ∈ A, transition P(s'|s,a), reward R(s,a,s'), discount γ ∈ [0,1].

Policy π(a|s): probability of taking action a in state s. Objective: max expected discounted return E[Σ γᵗ · R_t].

Value Function V^π(s): expected return starting from s, following π. V^π(s) = E[Σ γᵗ · R_t | s₀ = s; π]

Action-Value (Q-function) Q^π(s,a): expected return starting from s, taking a, then following π.

📋 Bellman Equations

Bellman expectation:
V^π(s) = Σ_a π(a|s)[R(s,a) + γ Σ_s' P(s'|s,a)V^π(s')]

Bellman optimality:
V*(s) = max_a [R(s,a) + γ Σ_s' P(s'|s,a)V*(s')]
Q*(s,a) = R(s,a) + γ Σ_s' P(s'|s,a) max_a' Q*(s',a')

📋 Dynamic Programming

Value iteration: V_{k+1}(s) = max_a [R(s,a) + γ Σ P(s'|s,a)V_k(s')]. Converges to V*.

Policy iteration: evaluate V^π (solve linear system), improve π'(s) = argmax_a Q^π(s,a), iterate.

🧠 Intuition: "The Bellman equation is a recursion — 'the value of this state equals the immediate reward plus the (discounted) value of the next state.' This recursive structure is the foundation of all RL algorithms."

📊 Concrete: "A GridWorld with 100 states, 4 actions, and 0.9 discount: value iteration converges in ~100 iterations (~0.5ms). Policy iteration converges in ~10 iterations but each solves a 100×100 linear system (~2ms)."
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# Bellman optimality operator — one step of value iteration
# V: array of shape (n_states,) — current value estimates
# P: array of shape (n_states, n_actions, n_states) — transition probs
# R: array of shape (n_states, n_actions) — expected reward per (s,a)
# gamma: discount factor

def bellman_optimality_update(V, P, R, gamma):
    """
    Compute V_{k+1} using the Bellman optimality operator.
    V*(s) = max_a [R(s,a) + gamma * sum_s' P(s'|s,a) * V*(s')]
    """
    n_states, n_actions = R.shape
    # Q(s, a) = R(s, a) + gamma * sum over next states
    Q = R + gamma * np.sum(P * V[np.newaxis, np.newaxis, :], axis=-1)
    # V(s) = max over actions
    return np.max(Q, axis=-1)

# Policy iteration: evaluate -> improve -> repeat
# Evaluate: solve linear system (I - gamma * P_pi) * V = R_pi
# Improve: pi'(s) = argmax_a Q_pi(s, a)

sections.title: Q-Learning & Deep Q-Networks (DQN)
sections.description:
📋 Q-Learning

Q-learning: off-policy TD learning.
Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γ max_a' Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]
Uses max over next Q — overestimates values (maximization bias).

📋 DQN (Mnih 2015)

Q(s,a; θ) approximated by deep network. Key innovations:

  • Experience replay: store (s,a,r,s') in buffer, sample random minibatch. Breaks temporal correlation.
  • Target network: θ⁻ copies θ every C steps. Uses θ⁻ for TD target: r + γ max_a' Q(s',a'; θ⁻). Stabilizes.
  • Reward clipping: clip rewards to [-1, 1] for consistent gradient scale.
  • Gradient clipping: clip gradients elementwise to [-1, 1].

DQN on Atari: same architecture, same hyperparams, 49 games. Human-level on 29/49.

📋 Improvements

Double DQN: y = r + γ Q(s', argmax_a' Q(s',a'; θ); θ⁻). Separates action selection (online) from evaluation (target). Reduces overestimation.

Dueling DQN: Q = V(s) + A(s,a) - mean(A). Two streams: value + advantage. Network learns which states are valuable without caring about specific actions.

Rainbow (Hessel 2018): combines 6 improvements (Double, Dueling, Prioritized replay, Multi-step, Distributional, Noisy nets). Best of all worlds.

🧠 Intuition: "DQN makes RL work with deep learning by adding two stabilizing tricks: experience replay (learn from past) and target networks (don't chase a moving target). Without these, the Q-network's own updates make the training target change every step."

📊 Concrete: "DQN trained on Pong: reaches 18+ score (beats CPU opponent) in ~500K frames (~4 hours on one GPU). Before DQN, no RL algorithm could play Atari from pixels."
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np
from collections import deque

# DQN agent with experience replay and target network

class DQN:
    def __init__(self, state_dim, action_dim, lr=1e-3, gamma=0.99,
                 buffer_size=100000, batch_size=64, target_update=100):
        self.action_dim = action_dim
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.batch_size = batch_size
        self.target_update = target_update

        # Online network — learns via TD updates
        self.q_network = self._build_network(state_dim, action_dim)
        # Target network — lagged copy for stable TD targets
        self.target_network = self._build_network(state_dim, action_dim)
        self.target_network.set_weights(self.q_network.get_weights())

        self.optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=lr)
        self.replay_buffer = deque(maxlen=buffer_size)
        self.step_count = 0

    def _build_network(self, state_dim, action_dim):
        return tf.keras.Sequential([
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu', input_shape=(state_dim,)),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),
            tf.keras.layers.Dense(action_dim, activation='linear')
        ])

    def act(self, state, epsilon=0.1):
        """Epsilon-greedy action selection."""
        if np.random.random() < epsilon:
            return np.random.randint(self.action_dim)
        q_values = self.q_network.predict(state[np.newaxis], verbose=0)[0]
        return np.argmax(q_values)

    def store(self, state, action, reward, next_state, done):
        self.replay_buffer.append((state, action, reward, next_state, done))

    def update(self):
        if len(self.replay_buffer) < self.batch_size:
            return

        batch = np.random.choice(len(self.replay_buffer), self.batch_size, replace=False)
        states, actions, rewards, next_states, dones = zip(
            *[self.replay_buffer[i] for i in batch])

        states = np.array(states)
        next_states = np.array(next_states)
        rewards = np.array(rewards)
        dones = np.array(dones, dtype=np.float32)

        # TD target: r + gamma * max_a' Q_target(s', a')
        next_q = self.target_network.predict(next_states, verbose=0)
        max_next_q = np.max(next_q, axis=1)
        targets = rewards + self.gamma * max_next_q * (1 - dones)

        with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
            q_values = self.q_network(states, training=True)
            # Gather Q(s, a) for the actions taken
            action_mask = tf.one_hot(actions, self.action_dim)
            q_selected = tf.reduce_sum(q_values * action_mask, axis=1)
            loss = tf.reduce_mean(tf.square(targets - q_selected))

        grads = tape.gradient(loss, self.q_network.trainable_variables)
        # ponytail: gradient clipping prevents divergence from outliers
        grads = [tf.clip_by_value(g, -1.0, 1.0) for g in grads]
        self.optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, self.q_network.trainable_variables))

        self.step_count += 1
        if self.step_count % self.target_update == 0:
            self.target_network.set_weights(self.q_network.get_weights())

sections.title: Policy Gradients & PPO
sections.description:
📋 Policy Gradient Methods

REINFORCE (Williams 1992):
∇J(θ) = E[Σ ∇log π(a_t|s_t; θ) · G_t]
G_t = Σ_{k=0} γ^k R_{t+k+1} (Monte Carlo return). High variance because G_t is noisy.

Actor-Critic: Actor (policy) + Critic (value function).
A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s) (advantage — how much better is a than average).
∇J = E[∇log π · A]. Lower variance than REINFORCE because advantage has less noise than full return.

📋 PPO (Schulman 2017)

Clipped surrogate objective:
L^CLIP(θ) = E[min(r_t(θ)·A_t, clip(r_t(θ), 1-ε, 1+ε)·A_t)]
r_t(θ) = π_θ(a_t|s_t) / π_θ_old(a_t|s_t) — probability ratio.
Clip ε=0.2 prevents too-large updates. Adaptive: if KL is too high, reduce LR (early stopping).

TRPO (Schulman 2015): constrain KL divergence between old/new policy. PPO is simpler (clip instead of constraint).

🧠 Intuition: "PPO's clip means 'if the new policy would make big changes for this state-action, take the conservative estimate of the improvement.' It's a trust region in function space — don't change too much too fast."

📊 Concrete: "PPO trained on MuJoCo: Hopper reaches 3000+ reward in ~1M steps (~2 hours). PPO is the default RL algorithm for continuous control because it's stable, sample-efficient, and easy to tune."
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

# PPO clipped surrogate objective (simplified)
# Assumes: log_probs from current policy and old policy

def ppo_loss(log_probs, old_log_probs, advantages, epsilon=0.2):
    """
    PPO clipped surrogate loss.

    L_CLIP = E[min(r * A, clip(r, 1-eps, 1+eps) * A)]
    where r = exp(log_probs - old_log_probs) = probability ratio.
    """
    # Probability ratio r_t(theta)
    ratio = tf.exp(log_probs - old_log_probs)

    # Unclipped objective
    surrogate1 = ratio * advantages

    # Clipped objective: prevents r from leaving [1-eps, 1+eps]
    surrogate2 = tf.clip_by_value(ratio, 1.0 - epsilon, 1.0 + epsilon) * advantages

    # Take the minimum — pessimistic bound
    # If advantage > 0: clip prevents ratio from going too high (over-optimizing)
    # If advantage < 0: clip prevents ratio from going too low (over-penalizing)
    return -tf.reduce_mean(tf.minimum(surrogate1, surrogate2))

# Actor-Critic forward pass (conceptual)
# class ActorCritic(tf.keras.Model):
#     def call(self, state):
#         features = self.encoder(state)
#         action_logits = self.actor_head(features)    # policy: pi(a|s)
#         state_value = self.critic_head(features)     # value: V(s)
#         return action_logits, state_value
#
# Training loop: collect rollout, compute GAE advantages,
# then optimize PPO loss over K epochs with minibatch SGD.

sections.title: Multi-Armed Bandits & Exploration
sections.description:
📋 Exploration Strategies

ε-greedy: with probability ε take random action, otherwise best known action. ε decays from 1.0 to 0.01.

UCB (Upper Confidence Bound):
A_t = argmax_a [Q_t(a) + c·√(ln t / N_t(a))]
UCB explores actions with high uncertainty (few trials). c controls exploration intensity.

Thompson Sampling: sample from posterior P(Q|data), take action with best sampled Q. For Bernoulli rewards, posterior is Beta(α_success+1, β_fail+1). Bayesian-optimal exploration. Better than UCB in practice.

📋 Contextual Bandits

LinUCB assumes linear reward function. Used in news article recommendation.

📋 Exploration in Deep RL

Noisy networks (learnable noise added to weights), entropy bonus (add negative entropy to loss, encourages exploration).

🧠 Intuition: "Bandits are RL without state transitions — you take an action, get a reward, and the world doesn't change. Thompson sampling is optimal because it automatically balances exploration (high uncertainty = wider posterior) vs exploitation."

📊 Concrete: "A/B testing with 5 variants and 100K users: Thompson sampling converges to best variant 40% faster than ε-greedy. Same final regret with 30% fewer samples."
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

# Thompson Sampling for Bernoulli bandits
# Beta prior: Beta(1, 1) = uniform on [0, 1]

def thompson_sampling(rewards_per_arm):
    """
    rewards_per_arm: dict {arm: list of rewards (0 or 1)}
    Returns: chosen arm

    For each arm, sample from the posterior P(reward_rate | data).
    Posterior = Beta(alpha + successes, beta + failures),
    where prior is Beta(alpha, beta). Using uniform prior (1, 1).
    """
    n_arms = len(rewards_per_arm)
    sampled_rates = np.zeros(n_arms)

    for arm in range(n_arms):
        rewards = rewards_per_arm[arm]
        successes = sum(rewards)
        failures = len(rewards) - successes

        # Posterior: Beta(1 + successes, 1 + failures)
        # Sample a plausible reward rate from the posterior
        sampled_rates[arm] = np.random.beta(1 + successes, 1 + failures)

    # Pick the arm with the best sampled rate
    return int(np.argmax(sampled_rates))

# UCB1 for bounded rewards [0, 1]
# def ucb1(rewards_per_arm, t, c=2.0):
#     n_arms = len(rewards_per_arm)
#     ucb_values = np.zeros(n_arms)
#     for arm in range(n_arms):
#         n = len(rewards_per_arm[arm])
#         if n == 0:
#             return arm  # try each arm at least once
#         Q = np.mean(rewards_per_arm[arm])
#         ucb_values[arm] = Q + c * np.sqrt(np.log(t) / n)
#     return int(np.argmax(ucb_values))

sections.title: RLHF — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
sections.description:
📋 Motivation

Language models trained on next-token prediction are good but not aligned — they don't follow instructions, can be toxic, and optimize for likelihood not quality.

📋 Three-Step Pipeline (InstructGPT / Ouyang 2022)

  1. SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning): collect human-written demonstrations for prompts, fine-tune GPT-3 on (prompt, ideal response) pairs.
  2. Reward Model (RM): collect human preferences for pairs of responses from SFT model. Train RM to predict which response is better. Binary cross-entropy loss:
    L(θ) = -E[log σ(r_θ(x, y_w) - r_θ(x, y_l))]
    175B GPT-3 → 6B RM.
  3. PPO Fine-Tuning: optimize SFT policy against RM reward + KL penalty. Objective:
    R(x, y) = r_θ(x, y) - β·KL(π||π_ref)
    KL penalty prevents too much divergence from SFT model (catastrophic forgetting).

📋 Alternatives to PPO-Based RLHF

DPO (Rafailov 2023): direct preference optimization — closed-form solution. No explicit reward model!
L_DPO = -E[log σ(β·log(π(y_w|x)/π_ref(y_w|x)) - β·log(π(y_l|x)/π_ref(y_l|x)))]
Simpler than PPO, equally effective for alignment.

KTO (Ethayarajh 2024): Kahneman-Tversky optimization. Only needs binary feedback (good/bad), not pairwise preferences. Even simpler than DPO. Uses prospect theory-inspired loss.

Reward hacking: the model learns to exploit the reward model (e.g., generate more verbose responses because RMs prefer them). KL penalty mitigates this.

🧠 Intuition: "RLHF separates 'what is good' (reward model) from 'how to get it' (PPO). The reward model embodies human preferences, PPO learns to satisfy them. DPO combines these into one step by directly optimizing the preference likelihood."

📊 Concrete: "InstructGPT 1.3B outperforms GPT-3 175B on human evaluations — RLHF makes models 100× smaller produce better outputs. GPT-4 uses extensive RLHF. Training cost: SFT ($100K compute), RM ($50K), PPO ($200K)."
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# --- Reward Model Loss (preference modeling) ---
# r_theta(x, y): scalar reward for response y given prompt x

def reward_model_loss(r_w, r_l):
    """
    Binary cross-entropy loss for preference pair.
    r_w: reward for the preferred (chosen) response
    r_l: reward for the rejected response

    L = -log(sigmoid(r_w - r_l))
    The RM learns to assign higher reward to preferred responses.
    """
    logits = r_w - r_l  # Bradley-Terry preference model
    # Equivalent to BCE with labels [1, 0] on logits
    return tf.reduce_mean(
        tf.nn.sigmoid_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            labels=tf.ones_like(logits), logits=logits))

# --- DPO Loss (Direct Preference Optimization) ---
# No explicit reward model — directly optimize policy from preferences

def dpo_loss(pi_logps, ref_logps, y_w_logps, y_l_logps, beta=0.1):
    """
    DPO: direct preference optimization.
    pi_logps: log probs from current policy (for chosen + rejected)
    ref_logps: log probs from reference (frozen SFT) policy
    beta: KL penalty coefficient (higher = closer to reference)

    L = -log(sigmoid(beta * (log_ratio_w - log_ratio_l)))
    where log_ratio = pi_logps - ref_logps
    """
    log_ratio_w = pi_logps - ref_logps
    log_ratio_l = y_l_logps - y_w_logps  # rejected ratio
    logits = beta * (log_ratio_w - log_ratio_l)
    return tf.reduce_mean(
        tf.nn.sigmoid_cross_entropy_with_logits(
            labels=tf.ones_like(logits), logits=logits))

# --- PPO-Objective for RLHF ---
# Maximize: r_theta(x, y) - beta * KL(pi || pi_ref)
# The KL penalty is estimated per-token or per-sequence

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Reinforcement Learning & RLHF
sections.description:
🔍 Interview Questions

Q1: "Explain the difference between value iteration and policy iteration — when would you use each?" — Google L4
A: Value iteration updates V(s) by applying the Bellman optimality operator repeatedly: V_{k+1}(s) = max_a [R(s,a) + γ Σ P(s'|s,a)V_k(s')]. It converges to V* in O(n²·k) where k iterations. Policy iteration alternates between (1) evaluating V^π for a fixed policy (solve linear system) and (2) improving policy as π'(s) = argmax_a Q^π(s,a). Policy iteration converges in fewer iterations (e.g., 10 vs 100) but each iteration is more expensive (solving a linear system). Use value iteration for large state spaces where solving a linear system is prohibitive. Use policy iteration for small-to-medium discrete MDPs where you want guaranteed convergence in fewer iterations. For continuous state spaces, neither applies directly — use function approximation (DQN, PPO).

Q2: "Why does DQN need a target network and experience replay?" — Meta L5
A: Without experience replay, consecutive transitions (s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1}, s_{t+2}, ...) are highly correlated — the network overfits to the recent trajectory, causing catastrophic forgetting of earlier states. Replay breaks this correlation by sampling uniformly from a buffer of past experiences, behaving more like i.i.d. training data. Without a target network, the TD target r + γ max_a' Q(s',a'; θ) uses the same parameters θ as the network being updated — this is a moving target. Each update changes Q(s,a), which also changes the target for the same (s,a) on the next visit. The target network θ⁻, updated only periodically, provides stable targets: r + γ max_a' Q(s',a'; θ⁻). This stabilizes training and was the key insight that made deep RL work on Atari.

Q3: "How does PPO's clipping mechanism prevent too-large policy updates?" — OpenAI L5
A: PPO clips the probability ratio r_t(θ) = π_θ(a_t|s_t) / π_θ_old(a_t|s_t) to [1-ε, 1+ε] = [0.8, 1.2] before multiplying by the advantage A_t. The clipped objective is L^CLIP = E[min(r·A, clip(r, 0.8, 1.2)·A)]. When A > 0 (good action), we want to increase π_θ(a|s), but the clip caps r at 1.2 — so we can't increase the probability ratio beyond 1.2× in one update. When A < 0 (bad action), we'd like to decrease r but the clip floor at 0.8 prevents it from dropping below 0.8×. The min selects the lower (pessimistic) bound, meaning we never take the optimistic estimate. This effectively creates a trust region without the complexity of KL-constrained optimization (TRPO). If KL divergences are too high, PPO also has an early-stopping mechanism that reduces learning rate.

Q4: "Explain the RLHF pipeline — what does each stage do?" — OpenAI L5, Google L5
A: Stage 1 - SFT: Collect a dataset of (prompt, ideal response) written by human labelers. Fine-tune the pretrained language model on this supervised dataset via standard cross-entropy loss. This teaches the model to follow instructions and produce well-formatted responses. ~10K-50K demonstrations. Stage 2 - Reward Model (RM): For each prompt, sample multiple responses from the SFT model. Human labelers rank these responses (pairwise comparisons). Train a separate model r_θ(x, y) to predict which response is preferred using Bradley-Terry loss: L = -log σ(r(x, y_w) - r(x, y_l)). The RM is typically much smaller than the policy (e.g., 6B vs 175B). ~100K-1M comparisons. Stage 3 - PPO: Optimize the SFT policy to maximize RM score while staying close to the original SFT model: R = r_θ(x, y) - β·KL(π||π_ref). The KL penalty prevents reward hacking and catastrophic forgetting. The PPO update uses the learned reward as the RL reward signal. This stage is the most computationally expensive (~$200K for GPT-3 scale). The result is a model that produces responses humans prefer, even though those responses might have lower perplexity than alternative outputs.

Q5: "Compare DPO and RLHF — what are the tradeoffs?" — OpenAI L5, Meta L5
A: RLHF (PPO-based): Requires training a separate reward model and running PPO with KL penalty. This means: (1) maintaining two models (RM + policy), (2) careful PPO hyperparameter tuning, (3) higher memory usage, (4) more compute (~$200K per training run). But: (1) the reward model is reusable for different policies, (2) PPO naturally handles exploration, (3) the KL penalty provides explicit control over how much the policy changes. DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): Eliminates the reward model entirely — directly optimizes the policy from preferences using a closed-form mapping between reward functions and optimal policies. Advantages: (1) much simpler — single-stage training, no PPO, (2) fewer hyperparameters, (3) lower compute (~$50K), (4) no reward model overhead. Disadvantages: (1) implicit reward may not generalize well to out-of-distribution prompts, (2) no separate RM for inference (can't score arbitrary responses), (3) assumes the preference data distribution covers the test distribution — can fail with distribution shift. Verdict: DPO is preferred for most alignment tasks today due to simplicity. RLHF/PPO is preferred when you need a separate reward model (for iterative data collection, guarding, or when compute isn't the bottleneck).

BONUS Q6: "Explain reward hacking in RLHF and how to mitigate it." — Anthropic L5
A: Reward hacking occurs when the policy exploits the reward model to achieve high scores without actually satisfying human preferences. For example, the RM might prefer longer responses with more bullet points, so the policy learns to generate verbose, list-heavy answers regardless of quality. This is a Goodhart's law problem: "when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." Mitigations: (1) KL penalty β·KL(π||π_ref) directly penalizes divergence, (2) ensemble of RMs during training (reduce overfitting to a single RM), (3) periodic human evaluation to detect divergence, (4) DPO bypasses this by removing the RM entirely, (5) iterative training with new preference data collected from the current policy.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Mnih, V., et al. "Human-Level Control Through Deep Reinforcement Learning." (DQN) — Nature 2015
  • Schulman, J., et al. "Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms." (PPO) — 2017
  • Schulman, J., et al. "Trust Region Policy Optimization." (TRPO) — 2015
  • Ouyang, L., et al. "Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback." (InstructGPT/RLHF) — NeurIPS 2022
  • Rafailov, R., et al. "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model." (DPO) — NeurIPS 2023
  • Ethayarajh, K., et al. "KTO: Model Alignment as Prospect Theoretic Optimization." (KTO) — 2024
  • Hessel, M., et al. "Rainbow: Combining Improvements in Deep Reinforcement Learning." — AAAI 2018
  • Sutton, R. & Barto, A. "Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction." — MIT Press, 2nd Ed.
  • Williams, R.J. "Simple Statistical Gradient-Following Algorithms for Connectionist Reinforcement Learning." (REINFORCE) — 1992
  • Lillicrap, T., et al. "Continuous Control with Deep Reinforcement Learning." (DDPG) — 2016
